mission.title: Install and Configure Redis Operator (Spotahome / OT-CONTAINER-KIT)
mission.description: The Redis Operator by OT-CONTAINER-KIT simplifies the deployment and management of Redis instances on Kubernetes, supporting standalone, cluster, and sentinel modes. It provides built-in monitoring and adheres to best practices for security and performance. This operator is ideal for teams looking to manage Redis workloads efficiently in a Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the OT-CONTAINER-KIT Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install the Redis Operator.

```bash

helm repo add redis-operator https://ot-container-kit.github.io/redis-operator

helm repo update

```

> This command sets up the repository for the Redis Operator.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the Redis Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the Redis Operator in the 'redis-operator' namespace, creating the namespace if it doesn't exist.

```bash

kubectl create namespace redis-operator

helm install redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --version 0.24.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the Redis Operator.
> Installs the Redis Operator using the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Redis Operator pods are running correctly in the specified namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator

```

> Lists all pods in the redis-operator namespace to verify the installation.

steps.title: Create a Redis Custom Resource
steps.description:
This command creates a sample Redis cluster configuration to deploy a Redis instance managed by the operator.

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
EOF

```

> Creates a Redis cluster with 3 replicas.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Redis Custom Resource
steps.description:
This command checks if the Redis instance is created and running as expected.

```bash

kubectl get redis -n redis-operator

```

> Lists all Redis instances in the redis-operator namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Redis Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Redis Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all Redis instances managed by the operator, leading to potential data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall redis-operator --namespace redis-operator

kubectl delete namespace redis-operator

```

> Uninstalls the Redis Operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Redis Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any important data. This process involves draining any existing Redis instances, performing the upgrade, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl get redis -n redis-operator -o json | jq '.items[] | .metadata.name' | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade redis-operator redis-operator/redis-operator --namespace redis-operator --version 0.24.0

kubectl get pods -n redis-operator

```

> Drains existing Redis instances.
> Upgrades the Redis Operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Redis Operator pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get redis -n redis-operator -o json | jq '.items[] | .metadata.name' | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRD'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Custom Resource Definition (CRD) may not be installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the CRDs using 'kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/main/deploy/crds/redis.opstree.dev_redis_crd.yaml'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Redis instance not starting'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient resources allocated for the Redis pods.

**Fix:**
Increase the resource limits in the Redis Custom Resource definition.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Pod is CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Redis pod is failing to start due to misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n redis-operator' for detailed error messages.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Redis'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Network policies or service configurations may be blocking access.

**Fix:**
Verify the service configuration and network policies to ensure proper connectivity.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Redis 7.x, improved monitoring capabilities with redis-exporter, and enhanced TLS configuration options.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.23.0, ensure that Redis instances are compatible with version 7.x.

resolution.summary: You should see the Redis instance listed with the status 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get redis -n redis-operator'.
codeSnippets:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis
  namespace: redis-operator
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources and the required tools installed.
